title: Chapter 32: The Foundation
content: Eight months after the Choice, the café has a second pair of hands.

She's a local girl — nineteen, quick with the espresso machine, quicker with a grin — and she doesn't need us hovering over her shoulder every minute of every shift. Which means, for the first time since we opened, the café can run without us for an entire day.
This freedom sits in my chest like a foreign object. A splinter of possibility I keep pressing against, testing whether it's real.
"We should take a day off," Julian says on a Tuesday morning, sliding a flat white across the counter toward me like this is a perfectly normal sentence that normal people say.
I stare at him. "A day off."
"That's what I said."
"From the café."
"From the café." He wipes down the steam wand, casual as anything. "That's generally what one takes a day off from, yes."
"We can do that?"
"She's here until five. We're not. That's the whole point of hiring someone."
I open my mouth. Close it. Open it again. He watches this performance with the restrained amusement of someone who has been waiting for exactly this reaction.
"I don't know what I'd do with a day off," I admit.

The words hang there, strange and honest and a little embarrassing. Eight months of building something from nothing, and I've forgotten how to be idle. How to exist without a task attached to my hands.
Julian reaches across the counter and takes my coffee away from me. "Then we'll figure it out."
We drive to the coast.

Not for any reason. Not because there's something to chase down or someone to meet or a multiverse crisis demanding our attention. There's just — the coast. It's there. It's summer. We have a car and a day and nothing else required of us.

West Cork in June is staggering. I'm not prepared for it, even though I've lived here through a full turn of seasons now. The cliffs rise up from the Atlantic like the earth is showing off, layers of green and gray and ancient stone all tumbling into water so blue it looks like someone adjusted the saturation. The wind carries salt and cut grass and something older, something that was here before any of us and will be here long after.

We park where the road runs out and hike along the cliff edge. The path is narrow, overgrown with fuchsia and gorse, and Julian walks ahead of me, his hands in his jacket pockets, his shoulders loose in a way they haven't always been. In a way I'm still getting used to.

We talk about nothing. Whether the cloud to the left is going to rain on us or not. Whether the sandwich shop in Skibbereen uses real butter. Whether cats understand the concept of a weekend. Important things. Life-or-death things.

We find a spot out of the wind — a dip in the grass just back from the edge where the land folds into a shelter — and Julian spreads the blanket he brought. Because he brought a blanket. Because this was a picnic. Because apparently we're people who do picnics now.

I sit cross-legged while he unpacks cheese and bread and a couple of apples from a paper bag. The cheese is something local, sharp and crumbly. The bread has a crust that crackles when I tear it. I eat with my fingers and let the crumbs fall where they want, and the food tastes like salt and fat and the particular miracle of not being in a hurry.

"Good day?" he asks, and there's something in his voice — not teasing exactly, but tender. Like he knows what this costs me, this unstructured time. This trust that the world will keep spinning if I'm not pushing it.

I look at him across the blanket: his hair messy from the wind, his jaw relaxed, his eyes the color of the sea below us turned warm by the sun. I look at the cliffs, green and gray and impossible. I look at my own hands, cheese-greasy and bread-crumbly and completely unoccupied with saving anything.
"The best," I say, and my voice comes out rough. "The most ordinary day in the history of ordinary days."
He reaches over and takes my hand. Just holds it. The wind moves through the grass around us like breathing.
I am so grateful for this I could cry.

We stay until early afternoon, switching positions on the blanket, watching the light change on the water. At some point we migrate closer to the cliff edge, our legs dangling over the drop, our backs against a boulder that's been here since before Ireland was Ireland. The wind picks up. I can taste the salt on my lips. Below us, the Atlantic throws itself against the rocks like it's trying to remember something.
"I've been thinking about my name," Julian says.
I turn my head. He's looking out at the sea, the wind pressing his jacket flat against his chest.

"Blake was my grandmother's surname," he continues. "I took it when I ran. It was — I needed something that couldn't be traced back. Something clean. And it worked. For three years, it worked. But it was never mine."
He picks up a pebble, turns it over in his fingers, throws it into the void. We both watch it disappear.

"My real name — Julian Gallagher — that's the name that was cleared. The name the records carry now. The name that's legal and free and actually attached to a person who exists." A breath. "I've been thinking about taking it back."
The wind fills the silence between us. A gull cries somewhere below, thin and keening.
"Do you want to?" I ask.
He's quiet for a long moment. Then: "I want to be the person my mother raised. Not the one who ran away."

Something in my chest cracks open — not painfully, but the way a window does when you've been holding your breath in a stuffy room and finally let the air in. This is it, I realize. The final step. He's been exonerated and he's been freed and he's been building a life here with me, but he's been doing all of it as someone who ran. Julian Blake was the identity that kept him safe, but it was also the identity that confirmed the running. Every time someone said "Blake," they said the name of a man in hiding.
Julian Gallagher is the name of a man who stays.
"Gallagher," I say, testing it. It sounds different. Older. Truer. "Julian Gallagher."
He looks at me sideways, and there it is — that crooked half-smile I've catalogued across a dozen timelines, the one that means he's feeling something he doesn't have words for.
"That's the one," he says.
"Then take it."

The wind pushes against us, and the sea crashes, and he doesn't say anything else. He just puts his arm around my shoulders and pulls me close, and I lean into him — into the warmth of him, the solidity, the fact that he is here and real and choosing to be Julian Gallagher on a cliff in West Cork with me.

And because he's holding me and because the wind is sharp and because there's something about the Atlantic trying to eat the land that makes honesty feel possible, I say something I've never said aloud. Not in the Garden. Not in any timeline. Not even to myself, fully, until right now.
"In the other timelines," I say against his jacket, "I loved the other versions of you."
His arm tightens around me but he doesn't interrupt.

"But I didn't choose you because I loved you more." I pull back enough to see his face. The wind has carved color into his cheeks and his hair is a disaster and he's looking at me like I'm the only solid thing on this cliff. "I chose you because being with you made me love myself more. That's what the Composite meant — choose where I'm most myself. And you —" My voice catches. "You make me the version of myself I actually like."
He doesn't say anything.

He just pulls me close again, and holds me against the wind and the sea and the impossible fact that we're both here, alive, chosen, real. His heartbeat is steady against my ear. His jacket smells like the café — coffee and warmth and him — layered under the clean salt of the air. I press my face into his shoulder and let myself be held, and the cliff holds us, and the ocean does what it's done for a thousand years, and this is enough. This is more than enough.
On the drive back, he sings.

Not guitar — just voice, filling the car with an old Irish folk song I don't know. His voice is untrained and rough at the edges, but it's warm, and it fills the space between us the way the café fills with steam in the morning: casually, inevitably, like it belongs there. The melody rises and falls in a pattern I can't predict, syllables in Irish I can't parse, and I hum along anyway — catching the shape of it even without the words.

The road unwinds ahead of us, narrow and winding, hemmed by hedges thick with honeysuckle. The sky is doing the Irish thing where it's three weather systems at once — sun on the horizon, rain over the hills to the left, and a strange golden haze directly above us that can't decide what it wants to be. The windshield wipers flick on and off as needed. Julian doesn't stop singing.
I watch the road unfold and I think: this is what Borges meant.
The Garden of Forking Paths wasn't a prison. It wasn't a trap or a test or a punishment, and it wasn't a cheat code either. It was a proof.

Every path is real. Every choice creates a universe — branches splitting and splitting and splitting until the math becomes unimaginable, until the map is more territory than the territory itself. But you can only walk one at a time. That's the constraint. That's the thing that makes choosing meaningful.

The others exist. In memory, in possibility, in the could-have-been. I can feel them sometimes — the ghost-weight of other lives lived and unlived, the versions of me who chose differently and are living those choices somewhere I can't reach.
And that's not loss. That's abundance.

I got to live them. I got to be the Avery who fell in love in a Dublin café, and the Avery who burned it all down in Berlin, and the Avery who chose coffee and cats and a man with calloused fingers on the southern coast of Ireland. Most people only get one timeline. One shot. One set of choices and their consequences, and no way to know what might have been.

I got to see the forks. I got to walk the branches. And I got to come back — not because I found the best one, but because I found the one where I was most myself, and that turned out to be the same thing.
The folk song swells on a high note. Julian's voice catches slightly, and he laughs, and keeps going. I hum along, off-key and content, and the road carries us home.
The café is gold in the evening light.

It hits the windows at a low angle, turning the whole front of the building into something from a painting — warm amber and deep shadow and the kind of stillness that only exists in places where people have put down roots. The sign above the door reads the name we gave it eight months ago, and the paint has aged just enough to look intentional.

The cats are furious. Minou is sitting on the front step with an expression of profound betrayal, and Breeny is visible through the window, methodically knocking something off the counter in protest. Eight hours. We were gone for eight hours, and they're acting like we abandoned them for a decade.

Julian — Gallagher now, Gallagher — opens the door, and the warmth hits me first. Then the coffee smell. Then the particular atmosphere of a place that's been lived in and worked in and loved in all day while we were gone. It wraps around me like the best kind of gravity.
He turns in the doorway, backlit by the glow from inside, and grins at me. That crooked, impossible, only-in-this-timeline grin.
"Welcome back," he says, "to our one and only timeline."
I step inside.
The café door closes behind me. One door. One threshold. One path forward.
It doesn't offer infinite possibilities. It doesn't fork or branch or shimmer with could-have-beens. It's just a door, into a place that's ours, in a life that we chose.
And that's enough. That's everything.
